title: Witch Week - Chapter 2 (Fragment 1)
text:
2

2

T HE NEXT DAY was the day Miss Hodge tried to find out who had written the note. It was also the worst day either Nan Pilgrim or Charles Morgan had ever spent at Larwood House. It did not begin too badly for Charles, but Nan was late for breakfast.

She had broken her shoelace. She was told off by Mr. Towers for being late, and then by a monitor. By this time, the only table with a place was one where all the others were boys. Nan slid into the place, horribly embarrassed. They had eaten all the toast already, except one slice. Simon Silverson took that slice as Nan arrived. “Bad luck, fatso.” From further down the table, Nan saw Charles Morgan looking at her. It was meant to be a look of sympathy, but, like all Charles’s looks, it came out like a blank double-barreled glare. Nan pretended not to see it and did her best to eat wet, pale scrambled egg on its own.

At lessons, she discovered that Theresa and her friends had started a new craze. That was a bad sign. They were always more than usually pleased with themselves at the start of a craze—even though this one had probably started so that they need not think of witches or birds. The craze was white knitting, white and clean and fluffy, which you kept wrapped in a towel so that it would stay clean. The classroom filled with mutters of, “Two purl, one plain, twist two . . .”

But the day really got into its evil stride in the middle of the morning, during PE. Larwood House had that every day, like the journals. 6B joined with 6C and 6D, and the boys went running in the field, while the girls went together to the gym. The climbing ropes were let down there.

Theresa and Estelle and the rest gave glad cries and went shinnying up the ropes with easy swinging pulls. Nan tried to lurk out of sight against the wall bars. Her heart fell with a flop into her gym shoes. This was worse even than the vaulting horse. Nan simply could not climb ropes. She had been born without the proper muscles or something.

And, since it was that kind of day, Miss Phillips spotted Nan almost at once. “Nan, you haven’t had a turn yet. Theresa, Delia, Estelle, come on down and let Nan have her turn on the ropes.” Theresa and the rest came down readily. They knew they were about to see some fun.

Nan saw their faces and ground her teeth. This time, she vowed, she would do it. She would climb right up to the ceiling and wipe that grin off Theresa’s face. Nevertheless, the distance to the ropes seemed several hundred shiny yards. Nan’s legs, in the floppy divided skirts they wore for gym, had gone mauve and wide, and her arms felt like weak pink puddings. When she reached the rope, the knot on the end of it seemed to hang rather higher than her head. And she was supposed to stand on that knot somehow.

She gripped the rope in her fat, weak hands and jumped. All that happened was that the knot hit her heavily in the chest and her feet dropped sharply to the floor again. A murmur of amusement began among Theresa and her friends. Nan could hardly believe it. This was ridiculous—worse than usual! She could not even get off the floor now. She took a new grip on the rope and jumped again. And again. And again. And she leaped and leaped, bounding like a floppy kangaroo, and still the knot kept hitting her in the chest and her feet kept hitting the floor. The murmurs of the rest grew into giggles and then to outright laughter. Until at last, when Nan was almost ready to give up, her feet somehow found the knot, groped, gripped, and hung on. And there she clung, upside down like a sloth, breathless and sweating, from arms which did not seem to work anymore. This was terrible. And she still had to climb up the rope. She wondered whether to fall off on her back and die.

vocabulary.definition: the state or experience of finding something funny or entertaining
vocabulary.example: A murmur of amusement began among Theresa and her friends.

vocabulary.definition: without expression, empty or showing no emotion
vocabulary.example: It was meant to be a look of sympathy, but, like all Charles’s looks, it came out like a blank double-barreled glare.

vocabulary.definition: unable to breathe easily; out of breath
vocabulary.example: And there she clung, upside down like a sloth, breathless and sweating, from arms which did not seem to work anymore.

vocabulary.definition: to hold on tightly to something or someone
vocabulary.example: And there she clung, upside down like a sloth, breathless and sweating, from arms which did not seem to work anymore.

vocabulary.example: At lessons, she discovered that Theresa and her friends had started a new craze.

vocabulary.example: Nan’s legs, in the floppy divided skirts they wore for gym, had gone mauve and wide, and her arms felt like weak pink puddings.

vocabulary.definition: feeling ashamed or uncomfortable
vocabulary.example: Nan slid into the place, horribly embarrassed.

vocabulary.example: Nan’s legs, in the floppy divided skirts they wore for gym, had gone mauve and wide, and her arms felt like weak pink puddings.

vocabulary.example: It was meant to be a look of sympathy, but, like all Charles’s looks, it came out like a blank double-barreled glare.

vocabulary.example: She would climb right up to the ceiling and wipe that grin off Theresa’s face.

vocabulary.example: She gripped the rope in her fat, weak hands and jumped.

vocabulary.definition: a tied loop or fastening in rope
vocabulary.example: When she reached the rope, the knot on the end of it seemed to hang rather higher than her head.

vocabulary.definition: to stay hidden or move secretly

vocabulary.definition: a soft, low, or indistinct sound

vocabulary.definition: tissues in the body that can contract to produce movement
vocabulary.example: She had been born without the proper muscles or something.

vocabulary.example: The classroom filled with mutters of, “Two purl, one plain, twist two .

vocabulary.definition: to behave as if something is true when it is not
vocabulary.example: Nan pretended not to see it and did her best to eat wet, pale scrambled egg on its own.

vocabulary.definition: deserving or inviting mockery or absurd
vocabulary.example: This was ridiculous—worse than usual!

vocabulary.definition: thick cords made by twisting fibers or strands
vocabulary.example: The climbing ropes were let down there.

vocabulary.definition: mixed and cooked quickly, especially eggs
vocabulary.example: Nan pretended not to see it and did her best to eat wet, pale scrambled egg on its own.

vocabulary.definition: to climb quickly, usually by gripping with the legs and arms
vocabulary.example: Theresa and Estelle and the rest gave glad cries and went shinnying up the ropes with easy swinging pulls.

vocabulary.definition: a slow-moving tropical American mammal or metaphorically a lazy person
vocabulary.example: And there she clung, upside down like a sloth, breathless and sweating, from arms which did not seem to work anymore.

vocabulary.definition: a smooth, confident manner of walking or moving
